quote: Hi. Is this Enrique? Yes. Who is this? Yeah. Hey, Enrique. This is, Tyke with Ion. I was giving you a call because I received a request for a better debt solar for the home.
stewart_read: The intro is garbled — 'a request for a better debt solar' is not intelligible as a legitimacy anchor. The schema's standard intro requires a clear reference to the form submission as a success signal. Tyke's phrasing fails that test. The customer's immediate 'Who is this?' suggests the intro did not land cleanly. This is a minor gap — Enrique recovers quickly and the call proceeds — but a clean intro would have eliminated the 'Who is this?' friction entirely.
alternative_hypothesis: The garbling may be a transcription artifact rather than a rep delivery failure. Without audio, this cannot be confirmed. If the audio is clean, the coaching implication stands.
coaching_implication: Pull the audio clip. If the delivery is as garbled as the transcript suggests, drill the standard intro until it's crisp. The first 10 seconds set the legitimacy frame — 'better debt solar' does not.

quote: I don't know. It didn't convince me.
stewart_read: Tyke captures that Enrique has seen prior designs and that they failed to convince him, but never asks what company or what specifically fell short. The schema's prior_designs verify item explicitly exists to surface competitive context. Tyke gets the timeline (two to three years ago) but not the company or the failure mode. This is a reactive miss — there is no evidence Tyke is deferring this to the closer strategically; he simply moves on.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Tyke may be avoiding a competitive rabbit hole that could derail the booking. Rejected — the schema flags this as a required verify sub-item, and the closer now walks in blind on what failed before.
coaching_implication: In the 1-on-1, ask Tyke: 'What company did Enrique use before, and what didn't convince him?' He won't know. That's the coaching moment. One follow-up question — 'What company was that?' — gives the closer a competitive anchor and gives Tyke a differentiation hook.

quote: So I'm I'm wondering if maybe this time there's better kind of solar panels or maybe the price has go cheaper or something like that, and it's a little more affordable than it was.
stewart_read: Enrique is doing the curiosity-creation work himself — he's already wondering if this time will be different, already generating hypotheses about improved panels and lower prices. This is a high-value signal: a customer who self-generates reasons to re-engage is primed for the closer's design reveal. Tyke responds by asking about timeline rather than feeding the curiosity. A suggested rephrase (not verbatim): 'That's exactly why it's worth taking another look — the design will show you what's changed for your specific home.' The hook is there; Tyke doesn't take it.
alternative_hypothesis: Tyke may be deliberately not over-promising on panel improvements to avoid scope creep. Partially valid — but feeding curiosity is not the same as making product claims. The schema explicitly calls for curiosity creation, not feature explanation.
coaching_implication: Show Tyke this moment in the recording. Ask: 'What did Enrique just hand you?' He handed a curiosity hook. The coaching move is to reflect it back — 'That's exactly what the design will show you' — without explaining anything. This is the objection_inversion principle applied to a positive signal.

quote: In order to qualify for the current programs, all we ask of the homeowner is you just have a credit score of at least 670. Is that gonna be an issue for you at all?
stewart_read: Tyke inserts the credit qualifier during the natural dead air of Enrique navigating the Dominion website. This is a competent efficiency move — a required beat completed without burning additional call time. The threshold stated (670) matches the schema. The framing ('is that gonna be an issue for you at all?') is appropriately low-pressure. No coaching gap here on the qualifier itself.
alternative_hypothesis: Reactive read: Tyke inserted the qualifier awkwardly mid-navigation. Rejected — the dead air is the right moment for a required beat that would otherwise interrupt the flow.
coaching_implication: This is a positive model for the floor: use navigation dead air for required beats. Worth surfacing as a 'what Tyke did right' moment in team coaching, not a gap.

quote: November or May 2025, six ninety eight kilowatts.
stewart_read: Enrique reads out a full 12-month usage history — May 2025 through April 2026 — including a February peak of 1,892 kWh and a July peak of 1,280 kWh. Tyke responds with 'Awesome. No. Thank you so much. That's exactly what we're looking for.' The bill is captured and immediately dropped. The schema's bill_anchoring section is explicit: capture alone is not sufficient — the bill must be flipped into emotional alignment against the utility. Tyke has the heaviest usage months in hand (February at 1,892 kWh, January at 1,750 kWh) and says nothing about them. A suggested rephrase: 'February at 1,892 kWh — that's your heaviest month. That's the number the design is built around. When the specialist shows you what that looks like on a solar system, it's going to be a different conversation than what you saw two years ago.' No dollar amounts needed — the kWh figure is the pain anchor.
alternative_hypothesis: Strategic read: Tyke is deliberately not dwelling on the bill to avoid over-promising before the closer's design reveal. Partially valid — but the flip is not a promise, it's an emotional bridge. The schema distinguishes between explaining features (scope creep) and anchoring pain (required).
coaching_implication: This is the universal floor coaching gap the schema flags: every rep captures the bill and none flip it. Use this call as the drill example — Tyke has the numbers, he just doesn't use them. The coaching move is: 'What's the one number from that readout you should have said something about?' Let Tyke find February.

quote: And then it looks like I have you down as the property owner. Is there anybody else with title with you, or is it just My wife. Your wife.
stewart_read: Tyke correctly identifies the wife as a co-title holder. However, he does not follow through on the schema's spouse_decision protocol — there is no attempt to include the wife in the appointment or confirm she will be available for the closer's call. The appointment is booked for Enrique alone at a time he selects unilaterally. The schema is explicit: co-title holders must be present for the close. This is a protocol violation, not an objection-handling failure.
alternative_hypothesis: Enrique did not raise the wife as a decision-making concern, so Tyke may have judged inclusion unnecessary. Rejected — the schema's protocol triggers on title status, not on whether the customer raises it as an issue.
coaching_implication: This is the clearest protocol gap on the call. In the 1-on-1, ask Tyke: 'Who's on the title?' He'll say the wife. 'Is she on the appointment?' He'll say no. That's the coaching moment. The fix is one sentence after 'Your wife' — 'Is she going to be available tomorrow at noon as well? We want to make sure she's part of the conversation.'

quote: The only things we ask of you is just with this phone call, you not be driving. Pretty self explanatory. We don't wanna be distracting you while on the phone. What number are you calling?
stewart_read: Tyke hits the no-driving anchor from the button_up protocol but omits two required elements: the one-hour block statement and the 'is that fair?' verbal handshake. He also omits the computer/email access anchor — he mentions email access at the end but frames it as 'make sure you have access to that' rather than the schema's 'you'll be able to sit down to review the design on your computer.' The booking is structurally present but the button-up is incomplete. The 'is that fair?' handshake is the schema's explicit predictor of show rate.
alternative_hypothesis: Tyke may have judged the full button-up unnecessary given Enrique's cooperative posture. Rejected — the schema marks these elements as required regardless of customer warmth, precisely because warmth on the setter call does not predict show rate.
coaching_implication: Walk Tyke through the button_up checklist against this call. He hit no-driving and phone number confirmation. He missed: one-hour block, 'is that fair?' handshake, and the computer-access anchor. These are mechanical — drill them until they're automatic.
